for the quick reply @jklymak ! You're right about the negative values but I was hoping that the top x axis would not go negative and start at 0, same as the bottom x axis. As you suggest the following works code works by specifying the limits (note that if I don't specify the right xlim, the top x axis is still not correct). However it doesn't have the nice buffer of white space on the left and right that we are used to. Is there a way to retain that nice behavior with `secondary_xaxis`? ```python fig, ax = plt.subplots(constrained_layout=True) xdata = np.arange(0, 11, 0.4) ydata = np.random.randn(len(xdata)) ax.plot(xdata, ydata, label='Plotted data') ax.set_xlim(left=xdata[0], right=xdata[-1]) ax.axvline(10, color="grey") ax.set_xlabel('X [m]') ax.legend() xnew = xdata**2 def forward(x): return np.interp(x, xdata, xnew) def inverse(x): return np.interp(x, xnew, xdata) secax = ax.secondary_xaxis('top', functions=(forward, inverse)) secax.xaxis.set_minor_locator(AutoMinorLocator()) secax.set_xlabel('$X_{other}$') plt.show() ``` Producing ","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/matplotlib/matplotlib/issues/comments/2455027774/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Closes 29067
Fixes a small [issue](https://github.com/matplotlib/matplotlib/issues/29067) with the secondary_axis tutorial where the interpolation from one axis to the other needs to be defined outside the bounds of the data that is plotted. This was previously noted in the tutorial but was not implemented correctly. The example in question has been simplified and updated so that the secondary axis is correctly derived from the primary axis.

Closes #29067
Fixes a small [issue](https://github.com/matplotlib/matplotlib/issues/29067) with the secondary_axis tutorial where the interpolation from one axis to the other needs to be defined outside the bounds of the data that is plotted. The example in question has been simplified and updated so that the secondary axis is correctly derived from the primary axis.

Fixes a small [issue](https://github.com/matplotlib/matplotlib/issues/29067) with the secondary_axis tutorial where the interpolation from one axis to the other needs to be defined outside the bounds of the data that is plotted. PR summary
Closes #29067
Fixes a small [issue](https://github.com/matplotlib/matplotlib/issues/29067) with the secondary_axis tutorial where the interpolation from one axis to the other needs to be defined outside the bounds of the data that is plotted. This was previously noted in the tutorial but was not implemented correctly. The example in question has been simplified and updated so that the secondary axis is correctly derived from the primary axis.

PR summary
Closes #29067
Fixes a small [issue](https://github.com/matplotlib/matplotlib/issues/29067) with the secondary_axis tutorial where the interpolation from one axis to the other needs to be defined outside the bounds of the data that is plotted. This was previously noted in the tutorial but was not implemented correctly. The example in question has been simplified and updated so that the secondary axis is correctly derived from the primary axis.

am simply re-running the code discussed in https://github.com/SciML/SciMLSensitivity.jl/issues/938 and implemented as a test here https://github.com/SciML/SciMLSensitivity.jl/blob/d1ded754fe3b0d505d91c8ab5ea728a90be56df1/test/local_sensitivity/complex_adjoints.jl

```julia
using SciMLSensitivity, OrdinaryDiffEq, Zygote, LinearAlgebra, FiniteDiff, Test
A  = [1.0*im  2.0; 3.0 4.0]
u0 = [1.0 0.0*im; 0.0 1.0]
tspan = (0.0, 1.0)

function f(u,p,t)
    (A*u)*(p[1]*t + p[2]*t^2 + p[3]*t^3 + p[4]*t^4)
end

p = [1.5 + im, 1.0, 3.0, 1.0]
prob = ODEProblem{false}(f,u0,tspan,p)

utarget = [0.0*im 1.0; 1.0 0.0]

function loss_adjoint(p)
    ufinal = last(solve(prob, Tsit5(), p=p, abstol=1e-12, reltol=1e-12))
    loss = 1 - abs(tr(ufinal*utarget')/2)^2
    return loss
end

grad1 = Zygote.gradient(loss_adjoint,Complex{Float64}[1.5, 1.0, 3.0, 1.0])[1]
grad2 = FiniteDiff.finite_difference_gradient(loss_adjoint,Complex{Float64}[1.5, 1.0, 3.0, 1.0])
@test grad1 ≈ grad2
```

This appears not to be a complex variables issue as it fails even if all complex numbers are replaced with floats, e.g. 

```julia
A  = [1.0  2.0; 3.0 4.0]
u0 = [1.0 0.0; 0.0 1.0]

p = [1.5, 1.0, 3.0, 1.0]

utarget = [0.0 1.0; 1.0 0.0]

grad1 = Zygote.gradient(loss_adjoint,[1.5, 1.0, 3.0, 1.0])[1]
grad2 = FiniteDiff.finite_difference_gradient(loss_adjoint,[1.5, 1.0, 3.0, 1.0])
```
